description: Lock organized_military_formations to the mid-seventh-century BCE Greek hoplite phalanx and its formation-specific material and historical lineage.

checks.reason: The former citation concerned Egyptian meteoritic beads, while iron was not the defining material system of the bronze-heavy hoplite formation.
Archers were auxiliary support rather than constituent ranks of the hoplite phalanx.
Writing preserved military culture but was not needed to assemble the formation, and standardized Homeric texts postdate its emergence.
Roads support campaign movement and logistics, not the battlefield mechanics of close-order infantry.
Mounted cavalry was a distinct arm from the foot soldiers who constituted the phalanx.
Earlier commanded shield-and-spear formations provide the appropriate historical lineage.
The ancient evidence establishes precedence without proving direct cultural transmission to Greece.
Bronze armor and the large shield formed the characteristic heavy hoplite panoply.
Bronze working enabled the panoply but did not alone determine coordinated formation tactics.
The civic organization lineage remains available transitively through organized_warfare without adding a redundant direct edge.
The reviewed emergence evidence favors about 650 BCE for the hoplite phalanx.
